content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
\JPractices Commission 
Kirk Alan Pessner 
Nielsen, Merksamer, Hodgson, 
Parrinello & Mueller 
July 22, 1987 
650 California street, Suite 2650 
San Francisco, CA 94108 
Re: 
87-203 
Dear Mr. Pessner: 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on July 21, 1987 by the Fair Political 
Practices commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact me directly at (916) 322-5662. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written 
advice. 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to the information needed. 
If your request is 
for informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we 
can. 
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Adm. Code Sec. 
18329) .) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
JP:jaj 
Very truly yours, 
. Jeanne Pritchard 
Chief 
Technical Assistance and Analysis 
Division 


LAW OFFICES OF 
NIELSEN, MERKSAMER, HODGSON, P,ARRINELLO & MUELLER 
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROF~!ONAL CORPORATIONS 
SACRAMENTO 
1030 FIFTEENTH STREET, SUITE 250 
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95BI4 
TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752 
Ms. Mary Ann Kvasager 
SAN FRANCISCO, C 
TELEPHONE (415 
July 17, 1987 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Post Office Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804-0807 
Re: 
Disclosure on Forms 461 and 465 
Dear Mary Ann: 
FILE NUMBER 
In our telephone conversation today, I advised you of a 
factual situation outlined below and you provided the following 
advice. 
Please review the question and answer as outlined below 
and if you believe the advice to be correct, please indicate so 
by signing the letter and returning it to me in the enclosed 
envelope provided. 
FACTS 
I advised you of a factual situation in which a 
corporation had made independent expenditures to qualify a ballot 
measure in a city. 
The amount of expenditures were in excess of 
$500. 
Since January 1, 1987, this same corporation has made some 
direct campaign contributions to candidates for legislative 
office, state constitutional office, county and city offices 
throughout the state. 
However, the aggregate of direct monetary 
and non-monetary contributions to all candidates during this 
calendar year does not reach $10,000. 
I asked you what forms 
must be filed by this corporation and where must they be filed? 
RESPONSE 
You advised the following: 
1. 
A Supplemental Independent Expenditure Report 
(FPPC Form 465) must be filed in the city where 
the independent expenditures were made to qualify 
the ballot measure. 
2. 
The Independent Expenditure and Major Donor 
Committee Statement (FPPC Form 461) would contain 
both the independent expenditures made in the 


Ms. Mary Ann Kvasager 
July 17, 1987 
Page 2 
qualification of the ballot measure in the city 
and all other monetary and non-monetary 
contributions made throughout the state. 
3. 
Since FPPC Form 461 discloses contributions and 
independent expenditures in more than one city and 
more than one county, the corporation qualifies as 
a state general purpose committee and would file 
the Form 461 on semi-annually filing deadlines in 
1987 with the Secretary of State, the Registrars 
of Voters in Los Angeles and San Francisco County 
and the County of Domicile. 
If I have misstated any of your advice, please do not 
hesitate to contact me. 
KAP:ryb 
Mary Ann Kvasager 
Date

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER, HODGSON, P,ARRINELLO

Date 
A 87-203

Under "Response," paragraph number 1, add the following 
language: 
1. 
Form 465 is filed at the same time(s) and 
place(s) as are the campaign statements 
filed by the candidate or measure supported 
or opposed by the independent expenditures.

sections.question_synthetic: A corporation seeks clarification on which disclosure forms (461 and 465) must be filed when it makes independent expenditures for a local ballot measure and direct campaign contributions to multiple candidates across the state, and where those forms should be filed.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The corporation must file Form 465 (Supplemental Independent Expenditure Report) in the city where ballot measure expenditures were made, and Form 461 (Independent Expenditure and Major Donor Committee Statement) as a state general purpose committee on semi-annual deadlines with the Secretary of State and specified county registrars. Form 461 should disclose both the independent expenditures and all campaign contributions made throughout the state.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains multiple versions of the same correspondence. Primary FPPC response is dated August 4, 1987 (Advice No. 87-203) with a clarification added to the original response. The incoming request from the law firm is dated July 17, 1987, and the FPPC acknowledgment letter is dated July 22, 1987.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What forms must be filed by a corporation that has made independent expenditures exceeding $500 to qualify a ballot measure in a city, and since January 1, 1987 has made direct campaign contributions to candidates for legislative office, state constitutional office, county and city offices throughout the state (aggregate contributions under $10,000), and where must they be filed?

CONCLUSION: 1. A Supplemental Independent Expenditure Report (FPPC Form 465) must be filed in the city where the independent expenditures were made to qualify the ballot measure. Form 465 is filed at the same time(s) and place(s) as are the campaign statements filed by the candidate or measure supported or opposed by the independent expenditures. 2. The Independent Expenditure and Major Donor Committee Statement (FPPC Form 461) would contain both the independent expenditures made in the qualification of the ballot measure in the city and all other monetary and non-monetary contributions made throughout the state. 3. Since FPPC Form 461 discloses contributions and independent expenditures in more than one city and more than one county, the corporation qualifies as a state general purpose committee and would file the Form 461 on semi-annually filing deadlines in 1987 with the Secretary of State, the Registrars of Voters in Los Angeles and San Francisco County and the County of Domicile.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to inquiry about disclosure filing requirements for a corporation making both independent expenditures for a local ballot measure and direct campaign contributions to candidates across California. The FPPC advises on proper forms (461 and 465) and filing locations.
